title: The Sonnets VI - Then let not winterï؟½s ragged hand deface
content:
آ آ آ آ Then let not winterï؟½s ragged hand deface,
آ آ آ آ In thee thy summer, ere thou be distillï؟½d:
آ آ آ آ Make sweet some vial; treasure thou some place
آ آ آ آ With beautyï؟½s treasure ere it be self-killï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ That use is not forbidden usury,
آ آ آ آ Which happies those that pay the willing loan;
آ آ آ آ Thatï؟½s for thy self to breed another thee,
آ آ آ آ Or ten times happier, be it ten for one;
آ آ آ آ Ten times thy self were happier than thou art,
آ آ آ آ If ten of thine ten times refigurï؟½d thee:
آ آ آ آ Then what could death do if thou shouldst depart,
آ آ آ آ Leaving thee living in posterity?
آ آ آ آ Be not self-willï؟½d, for thou art much too fair
آ آ آ آ To be deathï؟½s conquest and make worms thine heir.
meaning:
This evocative piece by William Shakespeare, titled "The Sonnets VI - Then let not winterï؟½s ragged hand deface", represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
